expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 12.00 cycles. At this rate:
  • 7% of peak load performance is reached (14.33 out of 192.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 12% of peak store performance is reached (15.67 out of 128.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

  • The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA (%R15,%RDX,8),%RDX] is unknown
  • The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA (%RSI,%R8,1),%R13] is unknown
  • The number of fused uops of the instruction [LEA (%RSI,%R11,1),%RSI] is unknown

0% of peak computational performance is used (0.00 out of 48.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
• Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Remove inter-iterations dependences from your loop and make it unit-stride:
    • If your arrays have 2 or more dimensions, check whether elements are accessed contiguously and, otherwise, try to permute loops accordingly: Fortran storage order is column-major: do i do j a(i,j) = b(i,j) (slow, non stride 1) => do i do j a(j,i) = b(i,j) (fast, stride 1)<>
    • If your loop streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA): do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)
gain.details:
All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is not vectorized.
Only 23% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 12.00 to 2.94 cycles (4.09x speedup).

gain.workaround:
• Write less array elements
  • Provide more information to your compiler:
    • hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Performance is limited by writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck).

header:
The loop is defined in /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:824-851. Analyzed code is defined in /home/lbw/architecture-opt-hpc-NPBMG/NPBx/OFFICIAL-NASA/NPB3.4.4/NPB3.4-MPI/MG/mg.f90:824,835,851. Warnings:
  • Non-innermost loop: analyzing only self part (ignoring child loops).
  • Ignoring paths for analysis
  • Too many paths. If you really need to analyze all of the 69 paths individually, rerun with max-paths=69
  • RecMII not computed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
  • Streams not analyzed since number of paths is unknown or > max_paths
Try to simplify control and/or increase the maximum number of paths per function/loop through the 'max-paths-nb' option. This loop has 69 execution paths. The presence of multiple execution paths is typically the main/first bottleneck. Try to simplify control inside loop: ideally, try to remove all conditional expressions, for example by (if applicable):
  • hoisting them (moving them outside the loop)
  • turning them into conditional moves, MIN or MAX
Ex: if (x<0) x=0 => x = max(0,x) (Fortran instrinsic procedure)
